item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Newton resident, +1 immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So in real life I found a statue buried very near to the bridge described in this SCP. I could go on about it. When I removed it, the very arbitrary line between cities was damaged. Bad things started to leak into Brookline. They were so very good at keeping them out before I found it. The statue does in fact attract the attention and following of non corporeal entities. It can be rather unnerving. I tried to sell or investigate how to go about selling it. 3000 dollars to authenticate it, seemed like a lot. So, now I'm stuck with an albeit attractive bronze that's worth a lot, I can't get it off without attracting the attention of unwanted authorities. I mean hey it was probably stolen but I didn't steal it I found it….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1206's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Apoplexic or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the quote formatting is fine as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the formatting could be updated. Requesting permission to remove the "" and add a > in the addendum's quote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one I like. Very different and original. If I was a cunning linguist I'd feel obliged to take a crack at a rudimentary version. As it is, were this invented, I'd learn it. Great feel to it all. +1

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded:
It's a bit confusing to understand the concept but I like it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah not all people are the same. I wouldn't expect Joe Sixpack to drop his jaw and start waxing poetic about a pretty sunset but frankly there's so many negligible, "back alley" criticisms you can make about every SCP on this site (and even actual literature. how the fuck doesn't Gabriel Conroy know about his wife's terminally ill first boyfriend? isn't it a little convenient he finds out about it on the exact night he first feels her beginning to drift away from him?) it's not worth the time to sit down and anticipate every one.

you can describe a rose in a thousand pages in 4 point font but none of it will actually capture the experience of a rose itself. not all people will respond to a generic beautiful setting like a sunset the same but all people have some conception of beauty and the sublime, and they experience it vividly when they encounter 1206.

the words are in the primordial language. i didn't feel like making up pretty-sounding words so there you have it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects have reported an overwhelming feeling of awe and the sublime, but have summarily failed to provide adequate descriptions of what they have seen.

Researchers have speculated that subjects have been unable to describe SCP-1206 because it appeals to latent forms in the human mind that cannot be accurately defined by formalized language.

>75% of subject groups will eventually fracture, as it is impossible to lie or be subtle when speaking 1206-A

subjects only report needing to meditate momentarily on the object of their description before inventing a word that "fits like a glove"

Every time I read a thing that goes like this "Subjects will always experience this and say this cheeseball line" I react with "Ur not the boss of me, come at me bro"

The point being one I remarked on a while back; not all humans are the same. You describe this flowery "beautiful" thing and say its indescribable. That sounds like a challenge to me, I'll describe it in one page or less. Why wouldn't I be able to? Because some memetic creature pokes at my brain when I try to describe it? When has a thing like pain or doubt or anxiety stopped a certain amount of people from doing something regardless?

And what possible words could have such a deleterious effect that they need to be censored and associated with a single SCP? Not phrases, but individual words. Is "deleterious" one of the 1206-A words? Curmudgeon? Philistine? Grouser? Lout? Boor? Gizzin? Gizzan? Gizun? Gizzee? Gizark? Fibbledy? Muklukklk? I'm implying I'm all these things since I clearly don't "get" the flowery beauty of it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very beautiful, and avoids so many horror cliches by going in the opposite direction of Evil SCPs.

I also like that the Foundation is showing its obsession with normalcy rather than Good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Between stops [REDACTED] the Green Line train passes through an open 0.4 km long expanse of trees and shrubbery bordering the eastern ridge of the town of ██████ classified as the Hot Zone, but is unnamed on any map."

As this is written now, the subject of the last clause ("but is unnamed on any map") is still "the Green Line train" when I'm pretty sure it should be "an open 0.4 km long expanse of trees and shrubbery…" Editing to add a "that has been" after the blackboxes; I'm a bit hesitant about how this works with the tone, but the sentence definitely does need to be altered in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oi, I'll fix that soon as I get home.

edit: done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As mentioned before, there is a watermark on the picture. Which is suffering from serious compression artifacts. Ugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what the containment procedures would look like if written in the affected language.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't comment on how it used to be, as I've just found it, but this is beautiful. It took me a little while to get there, and it's the first addendum that really carries the article, but… this is such a great idea. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just seeing this after extensive editing (and obviously a good few months passing), but why all the hate? I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like. The central idea is neat enough, but what really makes my brain giggle is wondering what would become of the world after the word-lies-are-no-longer-possible-ocolypse destroyed civilization as we know it.

In my head-canon, this would be the ultimate, best-case-scenario result of what AWCY? is trying to do. Bring about a singularity of social evolution by the literal extermination of hypocrisy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a watermark in the bottom right corner of the picture.

Also, like I said in the help thread however long ago, I like the execution, but not the idea so much. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That certainly would have made the work day pass faster too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It helps if you're stoned out of your mind. Shhhh…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure the stretch of greenery you refer to as the Hot Zone is the same one I used to pass through on the train when commuting to [REDACTED] Hospital [REDACTED]. It was pretty enough but I can't say it ever gave me a sense of the ineffable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's pretty good. Mind if I steal that line for the containment procedures? Totally okay if that's not cool.

Second, the beauty that triggers 1206-A is a primal beauty because it's the majesty of the sky, trees, and sun made all the more sacred by the fact of our residing in the world. In other words, the beauty is in the scene before us, and the knowledge that we exist to perceive it. I'm a big Buddhist, so if you're familiar with the discipline, you can see the influence of mindfulness and "looking with a child's eyes", of perceiving the world as deeply and free of the bs in our minds as possible. Of course, sitting around slack-jawed in wonder is no way to live your whole life. Sooner or later you have to get up and eat, which is why there's a sadness to this.

Also, there really is nothing stopping from 1206-A from being triggered by a more modern experience of beauty, like a symphony or a film, but that would make it nigh-uncontainable and some members here rightfully hate that. I decided to stick with a more natural kind of sacredness because our relationship with the world, especially the natural sphere, is where all art stems from to begin with.

Thanks for posting, you made very interesting arguments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but some tiny gaps in the plot won't leave me alone.

First, if there are other place/time/condition combinations that cause this, they should be mentioned in containment. Even if it's only a single line like "Location of additional instances is a high priority and each instance is to be managed on a case by case basis."

Second, and maybe more important, I feel like the location is really played up, but it isn't really the anomaly here. The language is the anomaly, and the lovely view is just what wakes it from dormancy. If I've got the concept right, 1206-A is inherent in all of us, and we revert to it when something is so "right" that we can't adequately describe it in any more modern language. I see no reason that a particularly moving symphony played in a precisely accoustic venue, or the taste of the exactly right ratio of apple pie to vanilla ice cream couldn't trigger 1206-A as well. If the language is a neck-breaking statue, the view is blinking. Does that make sense? I feel like I could use some 1206-A right now.

I really like this idea, and I enjoyed reading the article even with my quibles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well now, next time I'm headed to Newton, I'm gonna try and invent some new languages. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me, too. Very much so. Gladly upvoted. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this very disappointing. The buildup of the containment procedures and the precise conditions, the fact that it only affects people on a particular train, was all great. And then we get something that's happened before and by itself isn't all that interesting. I felt it was a major letdown. Not to mention I have no idea what "EMT" is supposed to stand for (Epic Meal Time?), and you should probably take out the watermark from the image. Man, old me had the worst taste in skips. I find myself with newfound appreciation for this article, four years later. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll admit I bristled at this at first, but I can't deny that this is a dressed up True Language. It nagged me when writing it and it nags me now. No sarcasm here, I'm glad you downvoted. I like that I can't run anything past you guys. It teaches me work even harder on my next SCP. I'm not going to settle for anything less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GRAMMAR IS SERIOUS BUSINESS GRAARRRGH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's your standard fantasy world True Language plus a wail about how the world used to be better before people defiled it by thinking about things. Downvoted so very hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads up, could have done without the attitude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing this gets you worked up a tad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*sigh* I'll say it again:

"Phenomena" is PLURAL. STOP FUCKING MISUSING IT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll get to that, soon as I figure out how to condense the writing. Article is dense as is, didn't want to smack the reader in the face with a text monolith right away. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, it does meander a bit. That'll teach me to follow my gut instinct next time.

edit: Mhm, I can definitely see why. Thanks. I'll work on this when I get back from school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not Pauli, but egocentrical enough to answer anyway, the writing was what turned me off the upvote at first. It was hard to get to the core of the text. But as a non-native speaker, I figure that probably doesn't amount to much in the way of arguments.

1206-A has developed concurrently even in isolated …

^^This is where I kind of got sidetracked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, thanks. :) Pauli, if you happen to catch this, I'm curious where you think the writing is hard to follow? I felt it was a bit dense myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now see, when I first read this I went 'meh' and continued on my way. That explanation made me upvote for great justice. And paradise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't blame you. The idea started out as something totally different and eventually developed into this. It feels scattershot even to me. I'm honestly kind of surprised the reception has been okay so far. Anyways, the basic gist is that when mankind was young, we were in "paradise". We hadn't 'named' natural phenomena through science or religion. Everything was a wonder, and we communicated that wonder in a language we were meant to speak, the seeds of which are in us all. But that beauty has started to recede, and now you can only find it in little pockets of the world when the the weather and timing is just right. It's a dying breed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops. I'll fix that thanks for the heads up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As exposed subjects feel a compulsion to communicate their experience of the Hot Zone, individuals will invariably develop a language (hereby referred to as 1206-A) that will replace all other forms of communication.

This makes it sound like they lose the ability to communicate in other languages, but (as far as I can tell) they merely greatly prefer to communicate in 1206-A.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or pretty much any other True Naming system out there. The Earthsea series is what came to mind most readily for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true, much of this is positive; however, as stated in the thing, it makes it impossible to lie. And not just lie as in "No, honey, I'm absolutely not sleeping with Sherry in accounting, I just have caller ID of her calling me 87 times because I've been working with her a lot lately!" It's an Absolute Truth sort of thing, so even something as benign as past imperfections that would have been forgotten/ignored becomes intrinsic to the very thing itself and is a constant reminder every time it's spoken about… And a wound that's constantly picked at will never heal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me way too much of the "Ancient Language" in the Inheritance Cycle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so far as i can tell, the effect this article has on me is the exact opposite of what it seems to be trying to describe. the writing is hard to follow, the effects aren't very clear, and the location is very specific, despite apparently not being unique. i know other people really like it, but somehow, NONE of it works for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (I hope I'm interpreting this right) How typical of the SCP Foundation to view something positive like this as a threat to Humanity. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this, but it really needs more containment procedures. Even ignoring the extra activation sites mentioned in addendum one, at minimum we need a policy for affected civillians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would presume it's a typo. A heTeronym is a pair of words that are spelt identically, but with different meanings and sometimes pronounciations. For example:

The D-class is a convict.

against

To become a D-class, a court must convict you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is frikin' excellent. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds dumb, i'm not a linguist, but what's a hereronym? Google reveals such word as not being mentioned much elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took something that people feel and made it into an SCP. The idea is absolutely astounding, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this article is that word that makes you feel sad and calm and warmly nostalgic at the same time.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you point me where the tone is off, exactly? And yeah, I'll try and condense this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It takes way too long to get to the point, and the tone is really off in places. Please make this more clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a little bit to understand the concept.

+1
